slides.title: The Two-Way Fork
slides.subtitle: Adding ELSE so exactly one of two blocks always runs, and determining the result of any conditional by tracing which branch the condition selects
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2023)
slides.script: Day 2 of Conditionals. Yesterday a plain IF gave you a one-way, optional detour: the block ran on true and nothing happened on false. Today we complete the construct with ELSE, turning that detour into a two-way fork where exactly one of two blocks always runs. Then we drill the second half of objective AAP-2.H — determining the result — by tracing which branch a condition selects, including the boundary values the exam loves. By the end you should be able to write an IF-ELSE and predict its output on any input, every time.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Write an IF-ELSE statement in exam pseudocode so that exactly one of two blocks runs, depending on the condition (LO AAP-2.H)
Determine the result of an IF or IF-ELSE by evaluating the condition first, then tracing which branch executes (LO AAP-2.H)

slides.script: Two targets today, both from AAP-2.H — the same objective as yesterday, now completed. First: write an IF-ELSE so that exactly one of two blocks runs depending on the condition — that is the 'write conditional statements' part, extended to the two-way form. Second: determine the result of any conditional, IF or IF-ELSE, by evaluating the condition to true or false first and then tracing which branch runs. Writing and determining the result are the two halves of AAP-2.H, and today they come together on the full construct.

slides.prompt: Yesterday's teaser, now decide it. The program runs: score ← 55, then IF(score ≥ 60){ DISPLAY("Pass") } ELSE { DISPLAY("Retake") }. Three classmates predict the output will be Pass, Retake, and 'both Pass and Retake.'
slides.subprompt: Who is right, and exactly why? Write down the value of the condition score ≥ 60 first, then the single branch that runs. Name the mistake each wrong classmate made.
slides.directions: Be ready to defend which branch runs and why only one does.
slides.script: Pick up yesterday's teaser and settle it. Score is 55, IF score at least 60 display Pass, ELSE display Retake. Three predictions: Pass, Retake, and both. A few minutes on your own — and do it the right way: evaluate the condition FIRST. Is 55 at least 60? No, false. So the ELSE runs and it displays Retake. The classmate who said Pass evaluated the condition wrong or ignored it. The one who said both imagined the IF and the ELSE can both run — they never do. Exactly one branch runs, chosen by the condition. That evaluate-first, one-branch idea is the whole lesson today.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 2 Guided Notes have 2 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: Adding ELSE — The Two-Way Fork
sections.description: Writing IF(condition){ first block } ELSE { second block } so exactly one block runs (EK AAP-2.H.3).

sections.title: Determining the Result
sections.description: Tracing which branch runs by evaluating the condition first, including boundary values (EK AAP-2.H.3).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. Absent or lost your packet? A digital copy lives on the Topic 3.6 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.
slides.script: Today's Guided Notes map: two sections, both green and CB-required. First, adding ELSE — the two-way fork, and how to write it so exactly one of two blocks runs. Second, determining the result — tracing which branch executes by evaluating the condition first, and handling boundary values cleanly. Keep the packet open; every answer also lives on the Topic 3.6 page, along with the interactive coding practice where you type and run these conditionals yourself.

slides.label: Adding ELSE — The Two-Way Fork
slides.kicker: Exactly One of Two Blocks · Learning Objective AAP-2.H
slides.script: Section one. Yesterday's IF handled only the true case. Add ELSE and you cover both: a first block for true, a second block for false. The College Board defines this precisely on the exam reference sheet, and the key property — exactly one block runs, never both — is what you must be able to state and use.

slides.heading: IF-ELSE: Exactly One Branch Runs
bullets: The exam reference sheet provides IF(condition){ first block } ELSE { second block } (EK AAP-2.H.3).
If the condition is true, the first block runs; otherwise the second block runs (EK AAP-2.H.3).
Exactly one of the two blocks executes on every pass — never both, and never neither.
ELSE has no condition of its own; it simply catches every case the IF condition did not make true.
slides.script: Here is the full construct, straight from the reference sheet. IF condition, then a first block in braces, then ELSE, then a second block in braces. The rule: if the condition is true, the first block runs; otherwise, the second block runs. The property to burn in is exactness — on every pass exactly one of the two blocks executes, never both and never neither. And note ELSE carries no condition of its own; you do not write a test after it. It is simply the catch-all for every case the IF did not make true. That is why an IF-ELSE always produces an outcome, where a plain IF could produce nothing.

capture.q: In an IF-ELSE, the second block runs whenever the condition is ______.
On any single pass of an IF-ELSE, the number of blocks that execute is ______.
capture.a: exactly one — never both and never neither

capture.q: ELSE does not test its own condition; it catches ______.
capture.a: every case the IF condition did not make true

slides.heading: The Second Block Runs on False
slides.caption: height is 44, so height ≥ 48 is false and the ELSE block runs.
slides.script: The full fork in two columns. Height is 44. Evaluate the condition first: is 44 at least 48? No, false. So the first block is skipped and the ELSE block runs, displaying 'Grow a bit more.' Change height to 50 and it would flip: true, first block runs, 'Board the ride,' and the ELSE is skipped. Either way, exactly one line prints. In Python the same logic uses if, a colon, an indented block, then else, a colon, an indented block — no braces, but the identical two-way choice. The pseudocode on the left is what the exam tests.

slides.heading: IF Alone vs IF-ELSE
slides.subheading: A plain IF may run its block or do nothing; an IF-ELSE always runs exactly one of two blocks. Choose based on whether the false case needs an outcome (EK AAP-2.H.2–H.3).

left.title: IF alone
left.definition: A one-way, optional detour: the block runs on true, nothing happens on false
examples: On false: no action, program moves on
Use when the false case truly needs nothing

right.title: IF-ELSE
right.definition: A two-way fork: exactly one of two blocks always runs
examples: On false: the ELSE block runs, guaranteed
Use when every case needs a defined outcome

slides.footer: AP TIP: If the question's false case must produce something, a lone IF is a bug — the missing ELSE is a classic exam trap.
slides.script: Two constructs, one decision about which to reach for. On the left, IF alone: a one-way optional detour. The block runs on true, and on false nothing happens — the program just continues. Use it only when the false case genuinely needs no action. On the right, IF-ELSE: a two-way fork where exactly one of two blocks always runs, so the false case is guaranteed an outcome through the ELSE. The design question is simple: does the false case need something to happen? If yes, you need the ELSE; a lone IF there would be a bug. The exam plants exactly this — a missing ELSE where an outcome was required.

capture.q: A plain IF may run ______ block(s) on a pass; an IF-ELSE always runs exactly one.
Reach for IF-ELSE instead of a lone IF whenever the false case ______.
capture.a: must produce a defined outcome of its own

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Write an IF-ELSE
slides.subheading: These map to LO AAP-2.H. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: Write an IF-ELSE in exam pseudocode that displays "Adult ticket" when age ≥ 18 and "Youth ticket" otherwise. Use the ≥ operator and braces.
Explain in one sentence why your statement can never display both ticket messages on the same run.
Rewrite this plain IF so the false case is handled: IF(balance < 0){ DISPLAY("Overdrawn") }. Add an ELSE that displays "OK", and state which branch runs when balance is 25.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 3.6 page.
slides.script: Stop and think, six minutes, full sentences. One: write IF age greater-than-or-equal-to 18, first block displays Adult ticket, ELSE displays Youth ticket — mind the braces on both blocks. Two: it can never display both because exactly one branch runs per pass; the condition selects one and skips the other. Three: take the overdrawn IF and add ELSE display OK; when balance is 25, balance less-than 0 is false, so the ELSE runs and displays OK. These are the write-an-IF-ELSE moves AAP-2.H tests, and you will type harder versions on the Topic 3.6 page.

slides.label: Determining the Result
slides.kicker: Trace Which Branch Runs · Learning Objective AAP-2.H
slides.script: Section two. Now the skill the exam scores most often: given a conditional and an input, determine the result. The method never changes — evaluate the condition to true or false first, then run only the branch it selects. Do that and boundary values, the trickiest inputs, become routine.

slides.heading: Determining the Result: Which Branch?
bullets: To determine an IF-ELSE result, evaluate the condition to true or false FIRST (EK AAP-2.H.3).
True → run only the first block and skip the ELSE; false → skip the first block and run only the ELSE.
After the chosen block finishes, control continues below the whole IF-ELSE.
Watch boundary inputs — the exact value in a ≥ or ≤ test decides which branch runs.
slides.script: One method, used every time. To determine the result of any conditional, evaluate the condition to true or false first — before you look at either block. If it is true, run only the first block and skip the ELSE; if it is false, skip the first block and run only the ELSE. When the chosen block finishes, control drops below the whole IF-ELSE and the program continues. The one place students slip is boundary inputs: with height greater-than-or-equal-to 48, the value 48 itself is true, so it takes the IF branch. The exact value in a greater-or-equal or less-or-equal test is what decides the branch, so test the edge deliberately.

capture.q: The first step in tracing any conditional is to ______.
capture.a: evaluate the condition to true or false

capture.q: When the condition is true, the ELSE block is ______.
capture.a: skipped entirely — only the first block runs
the exact value in a ≥ or ≤ test flips which branch runs

slides.heading: Same Code, Two Inputs, Two Branches
slides.caption: The only change is temp; the condition's value decides which branch runs.
slides.script: Same construct, and watch how the input alone decides the outcome. Temp is 70. Evaluate the condition first: is 70 at least 65? Yes, true, so the first block runs and displays 'Warm,' and the ELSE is skipped. If temp were 60 instead, the condition would be false, the first block skipped, and the ELSE would display 'Cool.' Nothing about the code changed — only the condition's value, driven by the input, changed which single branch ran. That is the entire skill: evaluate the condition, then follow the one branch it points to.

slides.heading: Trace the Branch, Line by Line
slides.context: For IF(height ≥ 48){ DISPLAY("Board the ride") } ELSE { DISPLAY("Grow a bit more") }, evaluate the condition for each height, name the branch taken, and give what is displayed.
slides.script: Trace the fork row by row. Height 60: at least 48 is true, so the IF branch runs and displays Board the ride. Height 48: the boundary — 48 is at least 48, true, IF branch, Board the ride. This is the row the exam targets, so say it out loud: greater-than-OR-EQUAL includes 48. Height 47: not at least 48, false, so the ELSE branch runs and displays Grow a bit more. Height 30: also false, ELSE, Grow a bit more. Every row, exactly one branch ran, and every row was decided by evaluating the condition first.

slides.heading: “Both Branches of an IF-ELSE Run”
slides.misconception: In IF(condition){ first block } ELSE { second block }, both blocks execute — or the ELSE runs as a follow-up after the IF.
slides.whyItHappens: The two blocks sit right next to each other on the page, so students read them top to bottom like ordinary sequential statements and assume both run in order.
slides.correction: Exactly one block runs on any pass: the first block when the condition is true, the second when it is false — never both and never neither (EK AAP-2.H.3). ELSE is not the next statement in sequence; it is the alternative that runs ONLY when the IF condition was false. Evaluate the condition once, run the branch it selects, and skip the other entirely.
slides.example: score ← 90, IF(score ≥ 60){ DISPLAY("Pass") } ELSE { DISPLAY("Retake") } displays only Pass. The condition is true, so the first block runs and the ELSE is skipped — Retake never appears. If both blocks ran, you would see Pass and Retake, which never happens.
slides.script: The trap that sinks IF-ELSE tracing. Because the two blocks sit next to each other, students read them like sequential statements and think both run. They do not. Exactly one runs: the first block on true, the second on false, never both. ELSE is not the next line in sequence — it is the alternative that runs only when the condition was false. The fix is the method: evaluate the condition once, run the branch it selects, skip the other. Concretely, score 90 with IF score at least 60 display Pass ELSE display Retake shows only Pass, because the condition is true and the ELSE is skipped. You never see both words — if you did, the construct would be broken.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Trace the Branch
slides.subheading: These map to LO AAP-2.H. Show your line-by-line trace on your Guided Notes.
prompts: Trace: n ← 12, IF(n MOD 2 = 0){ DISPLAY("Even") } ELSE { DISPLAY("Odd") }. Evaluate the condition first, then give the output.
For IF(price ≤ 20){ DISPLAY("Budget") } ELSE { DISPLAY("Premium") }, give the output when price = 20 and when price = 21, and explain what the boundary shows.
A classmate insists the statement in prompt 2 could display both "Budget" and "Premium" for one price. In one sentence, explain why that is impossible.
slides.directions: Show every step. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 3.6 page.
slides.script: Trace it, six minutes, show every step. One: n is 12, condition n MOD 2 equals 0 — 12 MOD 2 is 0, 0 equals 0 is true, so it displays Even. Two: price less-than-or-equal-to 20 — at price 20, 20 is at most 20, true, displays Budget; at price 21, false, displays Premium; the boundary shows less-or-equal includes 20. Three: it can never display both because exactly one branch runs per pass — the condition selects one and the other is skipped. Answers are reviewed as a class before the exit.

slides.heading: Common AP Traps
slides.subheading: Three ways Topic 3.6 loses points on the exam — one minute now, real points in May.

strategies.name: A plain IF does nothing on false
strategies.text: IF(condition){ block } with no ELSE takes NO action when the condition is false — no default, no error, just skip and continue. Do not imagine a hidden 'otherwise.' A missing ELSE where the false case needed an outcome is a bug.

strategies.name: IF-ELSE runs exactly one block
strategies.text: Never both, never neither. Evaluate the condition once, run the branch it selects, and skip the other. If a trace shows both blocks' output, the trace is wrong.

strategies.name: ≥ and ≤ include the boundary
strategies.text: height ≥ 48 is TRUE at exactly 48; price ≤ 20 is true at exactly 20. Confusing strict (>, <) with inclusive (≥, ≤) flips the branch at the edge value — the exact case the exam tests.

slides.script: One minute, three traps, real points. Trap one: a plain IF does nothing on false — no default, no error, it just skips and continues, so never invent a hidden otherwise. Trap two: an IF-ELSE runs exactly one block, never both and never neither; evaluate the condition once and follow the single branch it selects. Trap three: greater-or-equal and less-or-equal include the boundary — height at least 48 is true at exactly 48 — so do not confuse strict with inclusive comparisons, because that flips the branch at the edge value the exam always probes. Dodge these three and every conditional question on the exam is free points.

slides.heading: Conditionals, in One Slide
points: Selection determines which parts of an algorithm run based on a condition being true or false, and can be expressed with no code — plain language or a flowchart (EK AAP-2.G.1).
A conditional statement changes the sequential flow of control based on a Boolean expression (EK AAP-2.H.1).
IF(condition){ block } runs the block on true and takes NO action on false (EK AAP-2.H.2).
IF(condition){ first block } ELSE { second block } runs exactly one block — the first on true, the second otherwise; trace by evaluating the condition first (EK AAP-2.H.3).
slides.next: Topic 3.7, Nested Conditionals: now that one condition can choose a branch, what happens when a conditional lives INSIDE another block — how do you trace decisions within decisions?
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide. Selection determines which parts of an algorithm run based on a true-or-false condition, and you can express it with no code at all. A conditional statement changes the flow of control based on a Boolean expression. A plain IF runs its block on true and does nothing on false. And IF-ELSE runs exactly one of two blocks — the first on true, the second otherwise — which you determine by evaluating the condition first. Next class is Topic 3.7, Nested Conditionals, where a conditional lives inside another block and you trace decisions within decisions. Before you pack up, run the exit check: four 'I can' statements at the bottom of the slide, and any box you cannot honestly check tells you what to review on the Topic 3.6 page tonight, including the interactive coding practice. Guided Notes are collected at the end of class.
ican: express a selection decision without any programming language, naming the condition and both branches (LO AAP-2.G)
write an IF statement and determine its result — block runs on true, no action on false (LO AAP-2.H)
write an IF-ELSE so exactly one of two blocks runs depending on the condition (LO AAP-2.H)
determine the result of any conditional by evaluating the condition first, then tracing the branch (LO AAP-2.H)
